label: ai
topic: Prisoners sentenced to eternal life are orbited around a planet in spacesuits
text:
1.

The transport vessel Contrition enters the debris field at standard approach velocity. Inside the hold, forty-three prisoners are secured in transit harnesses. The field impacts register first as a low drumming against the hull, then as a continuous roar. Several prisoners flinch. One begins to pray.

The pilot, Officer Vasik, does not adjust course. She has made this run seventeen times. She knows that the debris is not rock.

"What is that sound?" a prisoner asks.

"That's them," Vasik says. She does not elaborate.

2.

The sentencing is brief. The Magistrate reads the charges in alphabetical order. Sedition. Conspiracy against the Protectorate. Distribution of prohibited texts. Unauthorised assembly. The sentences are identical for each charge, for each prisoner, for every transport that has come before and every transport that will come after.

The punishment for treason against the Protectorate is life. The Magistrate does not specify a duration. There is no need.

3.

The Renewal Facility occupies a concrete structure at the planet's equator. The prisoners enter through a steel door that opens once per decade and spend eleven hours inside. What happens in those eleven hours is documented in technical manuals that no prisoner will ever read.

The nanomachines enter through the blood. They repair cellular damage, extend telomere length, rebuild degraded neural pathways. They strengthen bone density and restore organ function to peak biological parameters. The process is painful in the way that growth is painful: a deep, structural ache that radiates outward from the marrow.

The final injection targets the voluntary motor cortex. After this, the prisoners can move their eyes. They can breathe. They can feel every sensation across every square centimetre of their skin. They cannot move.

A technician records this as "successful immobilisation" and marks the form complete.

4.

The suits are well-made. The Protectorate does not economise on punishment. Each suit contains a closed-loop respiratory system rated for two hundred standard years, a nutrient recycling module that converts waste products into caloric sustenance, and a temperature regulation layer that maintains core body heat across a range of negative two hundred to positive one hundred and fifty degrees.

The suits do not contain communication equipment. There is no one to communicate with.

A mechanical arm lifts each suited body and stacks it in the transport hold. The bodies are arranged efficiently, limbs interlocking, faceplates aligned in the same direction. From above, they look like a tiled floor.

5.

Officer Vasik opens the cargo bay doors as the Contrition passes through the orbital band. The bodies drift out in a slow cascade, tumbling gently in the planet's weak gravity, spreading apart as they find their trajectories.

Below them and above them and beside them, the ring extends in every direction. Billions of white suits catch the light of the system's star, each one rotating slowly on its own axis. Inside each suit, a pair of eyes is open. Inside each suit, a mind is aware.

The new prisoners see the ring for the first time through their faceplates. They see the scale of it. They understand what life means.

They will orbit for centuries. They will watch the star rise and set through visors that will not fog because the suits are well-made. They will feel their skin pressed against the suit lining and the slow pulse of the nutrient feed and the recycled air moving through their lungs. They will count orbits until counting loses meaning.

Some will pray for a collision. Some will pray for a system failure. Some will stop praying.

When the next transport arrives in ten years, they will feel the hull impacts through the debris field and will understand what the drumming sound was. They will hope the ship strikes them at sufficient velocity to breach the suit.

Officer Vasik will try. She always does.
